section.title: The Thirty Sayings of the Wise
section.sectionRationale: A new collection begins here, explicitly introduced as 'the words of the wise' in 22:17, distinct from the one-line sentence proverbs of 10:1 to 22:16. Addressed directly to 'my son,' it gathers longer, paragraph-length admonitions traditionally counted as thirty sayings, 22:20, running through 24:22 before a short supplement follows in 24:23-34.
section.thesis: Wisdom is received by incline the ear to the words of the wise, not invented alone, and it shows itself in refusing to exploit the poor, in disciplined appetites and disciplined children, in patience with the wicked's apparent success, and in fearing the LORD and the king rather than joining the rebellious.
section.frameNote: Ten clusters spanning three chapters: the prologue to incline your ear; do not exploit the poor, the angry, or your neighbor; table manners and the futility of chasing riches; instruction, boundaries, and a disciplined son; do not envy sinners or join drunkards; listen to your father, and the deep pit of the adulteress; woe to those who linger over wine; do not envy evil men, for wisdom builds and strengthens; rescue the perishing and taste wisdom's honey; patience with the wicked, and fearing the LORD and the king.

verses.text: Incline your ear and hear the words of the wise, apply your mind to my knowledge,
for it is pleasing when you keep them within you and they are constantly on your lips.
So that your trust may be in the LORD, I instruct you today, yes, you.
Have I not written for you thirty sayings about counsel and knowledge,
to show you true and reliable words, that you may soundly answer those who sent you?

units.label: Do not exploit the poor, the angry, or your neighbor

verses.text: Do not rob a poor man because he is poor, and do not crush the afflicted at the gate,
for the LORD will take up their case and will plunder those who rob them.
Do not make friends with an angry man, and do not associate with a hot-tempered man,
or you may learn his ways and entangle yourself in a snare.
Do not be one who gives pledges, who puts up security for debts.
If you have nothing with which to pay, why should your bed be taken from under you?
Do not move an ancient boundary stone which your fathers have placed.
Do you see a man skilled in his work? He will be stationed in the presence of kings; he will not stand before obscure men.

units.label: Table manners and the futility of chasing riches

verses.text: When you sit down to dine with a ruler, consider carefully what is set before you,
and put a knife to your throat if you possess a great appetite.
Do not crave his delicacies, for that food is deceptive.
Do not wear yourself out to get rich; be wise enough to restrain yourself.
When you glance at wealth, it disappears, for it makes wings for itself and flies like an eagle to the sky.
Do not eat the bread of a stingy man, and do not crave his delicacies;
for he is keeping track, inwardly counting the cost. "Eat and drink," he says to you, but his heart is not with you.
You will vomit up what little you have eaten and waste your pleasant words.

units.label: Instruction, boundaries, and a disciplined son

verses.text: Do not speak to a fool, for he will despise the wisdom of your words.
Do not move an ancient boundary stone or encroach on the fields of the fatherless,
for their Redeemer is strong; He will take up their case against you.
Apply your heart to {{musar|instruction}} and your ears to words of knowledge.
Do not withhold discipline from a child; although you strike him with a rod, he will not die.
Strike him with a rod, and you will deliver his soul from Sheol.
My son, if your heart is wise, my own heart will indeed rejoice.
My inmost being will rejoice when your lips speak what is right.
Do not let your heart envy sinners, but always continue in the fear of the LORD.
For surely there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
Listen, my son, and be wise, and guide your heart on the right course.
Do not join those who drink too much wine or gorge themselves on meat.
For the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty, and drowsiness will clothe them in rags.
Listen to your father who gave you life, and do not despise your mother when she is old.
Invest in truth and never sell it, in wisdom and instruction and understanding.
The father of a righteous man will greatly rejoice, and he who fathers a wise son will delight in him.
May your father and mother be glad, and may she who gave you birth rejoice!
My son, give me your {{leb|heart}}, and let your eyes delight in my ways.
For a prostitute is a deep pit, and an adulteress is a narrow well.
Like a robber she lies in wait and multiplies the faithless among men.

units.label: Woe to those who linger over wine

verses.text: Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Who has contentions? Who has complaints? Who has needless wounds? Who has bloodshot eyes?
Those who linger over wine, who go to taste mixed drinks.
Do not gaze at wine while it is red, when it sparkles in the cup and goes down smoothly.
In the end it bites like a snake and stings like a viper.
Your eyes will see strange things, and your mind will utter perversities.
You will be like one sleeping on the high seas or lying on the top of a mast:
"They struck me, but I feel no pain! They beat me, but I did not know it! When can I wake up to search for another drink?"

units.label: Do not envy evil men, for wisdom builds

verses.text: Do not envy wicked men or desire their company;
for their hearts devise violence, and their lips declare trouble.
By {{chokmah|wisdom}} a house is built and by understanding it is established;
through knowledge its rooms are filled with every precious and beautiful treasure.
A wise man is strong, and a man of knowledge enhances his strength.
Only with sound guidance should you wage war, and victory lies in a multitude of counselors.
Wisdom is too high for a fool; he does not open his mouth in the meeting place.
He who plots evil will be called a schemer.
A foolish scheme is sin, and a mocker is detestable to men.

units.label: Rescue the perishing, taste wisdom's honey

verses.text: If you faint in the day of distress, how small is your strength!
Rescue those being led away to death, and restrain those stumbling toward the slaughter.
If you say, "Behold, we did not know about this," does not He who weighs hearts consider it? Does not the One who guards your life know? Will He not repay a man according to his deeds?
Eat honey, my son, for it is good, and the honeycomb is sweet to your taste.
Know therefore that wisdom is sweet to your soul. If you find it, there is a future for you, and your hope will never be cut off.

units.label: Patience with the wicked, fearing the LORD and the king

verses.text: Do not lie in wait, O wicked man, near the dwelling of the righteous; do not destroy his resting place.
For though a righteous man may fall seven times, he still gets up; but the wicked stumble in bad times.
Do not gloat when your enemy falls, and do not let your heart rejoice when he stumbles,
or the LORD will see and disapprove, and turn His wrath away from him.
Do not fret over evildoers, and do not be envious of the wicked.
For the evil man has no future; the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished.
My son, fear the LORD and the king, and do not associate with the rebellious.
For they will bring sudden destruction. Who knows what ruin they can bring?

musar.gloss: Instruction, discipline, moral correction. Verse 23:12 calls the son to apply his heart to it, the same word used for the parental discipline of 23:13, showing that both formal teaching and corrective discipline share one Hebrew root: shaping character through received correction, not self-invention.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, the skill of ordering life rightly. Verse 24:3 says by wisdom a house is built, a domestic image for how wisdom is not abstract theory but the practical skill that establishes, fills, and sustains a real household, echoing wisdom's larger role in building creation itself, Proverbs 3:19-20.

leb.gloss: Heart, the seat of will, thought, and desire in Hebrew thought, broader than mere emotion. The father's plea in 23:26, 'give me your heart,' asks for the whole inner allegiance of the son, not mere outward compliance, an appeal Scripture applies ultimately to the human heart's response to God.

basic.context: A new section begins here, explicitly called 'the words of the wise,' distinct from the shorter one-line proverbs before it. Addressed directly to 'my son,' it strings together thirty longer admonitions, 22:20, covering everything from not exploiting the poor to refusing envy of the wicked, disciplining children, resisting gluttony and drunkenness, and fearing the LORD and the king rather than joining rebels.
basic.hermeneutics: This is instructional wisdom literature, using direct address and longer paragraphs rather than isolated one-liners, and its commands function as real, binding wisdom for a covenant people even though, like all Proverbs, they describe the normal path of wisdom rather than mechanical guarantees for every case. The famous line, 'have I not written for you thirty sayings,' 22:20, gives this whole unit its name and its scope through 24:22.
basic.originalLanguages: Musar, instruction or discipline, verse 23:12, links formal teaching with the corrective discipline of a child, verse 23:13, one Hebrew root binding both together as parts of the same formative process. Chokmah, wisdom, verse 24:3, is pictured building a literal house, grounding wisdom in practical, domestic skill rather than abstract theory. And leb, heart, verse 23:26, is what the father ultimately asks his son to surrender, the whole inner self, not mere outward behavior.
basic.typology: Wisdom building a house, verse 24:3, anticipates Christ, the wisdom of God through whom all things were made, John 1:3, and who builds His church as a spiritual house on Himself as the cornerstone, 1 Peter 2:4-5. The plea, 'my son, give me your heart,' verse 23:26, anticipates the gospel's call for total allegiance to God, not mere external religion.
basic.christ: This section repeatedly commends wisdom as the builder of a secure house, verse 24:3, a truth fulfilled in Christ, through whom all things were made and who now builds His church as a living house on Himself, John 1:3, 1 Peter 2:4-5. And the father's tender appeal, 'my son, give me your heart,' verse 23:26, anticipates the greater appeal of the gospel, which asks not mere outward conformity but the whole heart's allegiance to God through faith in His Son.
basic.god: God is the defender of the poor and the fatherless, taking up their case Himself, verses 22:23 and 23:11, and He weighs hearts and repays every person according to deeds, verse 24:12, so that no claim of ignorance excuses failing to rescue those in danger. He also disapproves of gloating over a fallen enemy, verse 24:17-18, revealing a character that tempers justice with restraint even toward the wicked.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:3 and 1 Peter 2:4-5 echo wisdom building a house in 24:3. Deuteronomy 19:14 and 27:17 echo the warning against moving ancient boundary stones. Romans 12:19-21 echoes the warning against gloating over a fallen enemy, 24:17. Hebrews 12:5-11 develops the discipline of a son commended in 23:13-14.
basic.oneStory: The thirty sayings call God's people to receive wisdom humbly, refuse to exploit the vulnerable, discipline appetite and children alike, and trust the LORD's justice rather than envying the wicked or gloating over their fall. This whole way of wise living is fulfilled in Christ, the wisdom of God who builds His people into a lasting house and asks for nothing less than the whole heart.

context.literary: A distinct section, 22:17 to 24:22, marked off by its own introduction, 22:17-21, its second-person address, 'my son,' and its longer, paragraph-style admonitions rather than isolated one-line proverbs, traditionally counted as thirty sayings, 22:20, before a short supplement follows in 24:23-34.
context.historical: Many scholars have long noted striking parallels between this section, especially 22:17-23:11, and the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope, an older wisdom text also organized in about thirty sayings; ancient Near Eastern wisdom shared a common stock of practical, international moral teaching that inspired biblical writers could take up, refine, and ground in covenant with the LORD.
context.canonical: This section's warnings against greed, its call to rescue the perishing, and its vision of wisdom building a lasting house echo through the rest of Scripture's wisdom and prophetic tradition, and anticipate the New Testament's fuller revelation of Christ as the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To instruct a son directly in practical wisdom, protecting the vulnerable, disciplining appetite and character, refusing envy of the wicked, and fearing the LORD and rightful authority, through thirty memorable, longer admonitions rather than one-line proverbs.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Nearly all thirty sayings are direct commands, do not rob the poor, do not envy sinners, rescue those led to death, so this section reads more like instruction than mere observation; even so, verses describing consequences, such as 24:20, 'the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished,' function as grounding motivation rather than commands themselves.

debates.question: What should be made of this section's close parallels to the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope?

views.label: Direct literary borrowing that undermines the section's unique inspiration
views.case: Some critical scholars argue the close structural and thematic parallels, especially the shared 'thirty sayings' framework, show Proverbs directly copied an Egyptian source, raising questions about originality and inspiration.

views.label: Shared ancient Near Eastern wisdom idiom taken up and transformed under inspiration
views.case: Scripture elsewhere draws on common ancient literary forms, covenant treaty structures, legal codes, without compromising its own inspired authority; here the biblical author uses a familiar wisdom form and vocabulary but reorients every saying around 'trust in the LORD,' 22:19, and 'the fear of the LORD,' 23:17, giving borrowed material a thoroughly Yahwistic center absent from its Egyptian counterpart.

debates.bearing: Recognizing shared ancient wisdom forms need not threaten confidence in inspiration; Scripture regularly takes up common cultural material and fills it with covenantal, God-centered meaning, exactly as this section explicitly grounds every saying in trusting and fearing the LORD.

study.originalLanguages: Musar, instruction or discipline, 23:12-13, binds formal teaching and corrective discipline into one process of shaping character. Chokmah, wisdom, 24:3, is pictured building an actual house, filling its rooms with treasure through knowledge, grounding wisdom in tangible, practical skill. And leb, heart, 23:26, names the whole inner self the father seeks, not mere outward compliance but total allegiance.

typology.type: Wisdom building and establishing a house, 24:3
typology.fulfillment: Christ, through whom all things were made, and who builds His church as a living house, John 1:3, 1 Peter 2:4-5.

typology.type: The father's plea, 'give me your heart,' 23:26
typology.fulfillment: The gospel's call for total allegiance to God through faith in Christ, not mere external religion.

study.christ: Wisdom that builds a house and fills it with treasure through knowledge, 24:3-4, points to Christ, the wisdom of God through whom all things were made, John 1:3, and who is now building His people into a spiritual house with Himself as the cornerstone, 1 Peter 2:4-5. The father's plea, 'my son, give me your heart,' 23:26, anticipates the gospel's own appeal, that God does not want mere outward performance but the whole heart, an appeal answered only when the Spirit gives a new heart through union with Christ, Ezekiel 36:26, Romans 10:9-10.
study.god: God defends the poor and the fatherless as their Redeemer, taking up their case Himself against any who exploit them, 22:23, 23:11, and He weighs every heart and repays according to deeds, so that ignorance never excuses failing to rescue the perishing, 24:12. He is also patient and measured even toward the wicked, disapproving when His people gloat over an enemy's fall, 24:17-18, revealing that His justice is never paired with human vindictiveness.

crossReferences.note: Christ as builder and cornerstone, fulfilling wisdom building a house in Proverbs 24:3.
The law's own warning against moving ancient boundary stones, echoed in Proverbs 22:28 and 23:10.
Do not repay evil for evil, and do not gloat, echoing the restraint commended in Proverbs 24:17-18.
The Lord disciplines those He loves, developing the discipline of a son in Proverbs 23:13-14.

study.oneStory: The thirty sayings of the wise call God's people to protect the vulnerable, discipline both appetite and children, refuse envy of the wicked, and give their whole heart to instruction and to God. Every strand comes together in Christ, the wisdom of God who builds a lasting house for His people and who alone can win the whole heart the Father in this passage longs for.

quiz.q: According to 22:22-23, why should you not rob a poor man?

options.text: Because the LORD will take up their case and plunder those who rob them.
options.note: God Himself defends the poor as their advocate and avenger.

options.text: Because the poor have legal representation available to them.
options.note: The text names the LORD, not human legal systems, as the poor's defender.

options.text: Because it is simply bad for your public reputation.
options.note: The stated reason is the LORD's own defense of the poor, not reputation management.

options.text: Because the poor are usually able to defend themselves.
options.note: This misreads the text, which specifically names the LORD as their defender because they cannot defend themselves at the gate.

quiz.q: What does 23:13-14 counsel about disciplining a child?

options.text: Do not withhold discipline; striking with a rod can deliver his soul from Sheol.
options.note: Timely correction is presented as life-preserving, not merely punitive.

options.text: Discipline should never involve any form of correction.
options.note: This is the opposite of what these verses urge.

options.text: Discipline is only needed for children who are naturally difficult.
options.note: The text gives no such exception; it addresses discipline generally.

options.text: Discipline is helpful but not urgently necessary.
options.note: The verses' urgency, tying discipline to deliverance from Sheol, is stronger than merely 'helpful.'

quiz.q: What warning does 23:31-32 give about wine?

options.text: Do not gaze at it when it sparkles, for in the end it bites like a snake.
options.note: Wine's appeal is deceptive, ending in real harm.

options.text: Wine is harmless as long as it looks appealing.
options.note: This is exactly what the passage warns against trusting.

options.text: Wine should be avoided only in large quantities.
options.note: The warning is about being drawn in by its appeal at all, not merely quantity.

options.text: Wine is dangerous only for those already prone to addiction.
options.note: The text gives a general warning, not one limited to a particular predisposition.

quiz.q: According to 24:11-12, what should you do for those being led away to death, and why?

options.text: Rescue them, since the One who weighs hearts and repays deeds already knows if you claim ignorance.
options.note: God's own knowledge of hearts removes the excuse of claimed ignorance.

options.text: Avoid getting involved to protect your own safety.
options.note: This is the opposite of the command to rescue given here.

options.text: Rescue them only if you personally know them.
options.note: The text gives no such limitation; it is a general call to rescue.

options.text: Report the situation to authorities but take no direct action yourself.
options.note: The text's direct command is to rescue and restrain personally, not merely to report to others.

options.text: Gloating when your enemy falls, since the LORD may disapprove and turn His wrath away from that enemy instead.
options.note: Even justified satisfaction over an enemy's downfall can provoke God's disapproval.

options.text: Ever having enemies at all.
options.note: The passage assumes enemies exist; its concern is the response to their downfall.

options.text: Helping an enemy in any circumstance.
options.note: This is not the warning; the concern is gloating, not helping.

options.text: Feeling any emotion at all when an enemy stumbles.
options.note: The specific warning is against gloating and rejoicing, a stronger and more specific concern than 'any emotion.'

questions: The section opens by calling you to incline your ear to received wisdom, 22:17. What voice of wise counsel have you been resisting or tuning out lately?
Verses 22:22-23 warn against exploiting the poor because the LORD Himself defends them. Who in your circle of influence has less power than you, and how are you treating them?
The wine warning of 23:29-35 pictures a person so numbed they cannot feel the blows landing on them. Is there a habit or substance dulling your ability to feel real consequences in your life?
24:11-12 calls you to rescue those being led toward death, refusing the excuse of claimed ignorance. What need right in front of you have you been pretending not to notice?
24:17-18 warns against gloating over a fallen enemy. Who is someone you have recently seen stumble, and what would it look like to respond with restraint instead of satisfaction?

translationNotes.note: 'Thirty sayings' translates a Hebrew phrase whose consonants can also be read 'excellent things' or 'officers,' but the reading 'thirty' is widely favored since the section that follows can be counted into roughly thirty distinct admonitions.

'For he is keeping track, inwardly counting the cost' renders a difficult Hebrew idiom sometimes translated 'as he calculates within himself,' describing a stingy host who resents every bite his guest takes.

'Though a righteous man may fall seven times, he still gets up' describes repeated setbacks overcome by resilience, not sinless perfection; the number seven functions idiomatically for completeness or repetition.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 22:17-24:22, spanning three chapters. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
